title: Kassel

infobox.key: • [City](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Germany_by_population "List of cities in Germany by population")

infobox.value: (German pronunciation: [[ˈkasl̩]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Kassel.ogg "File:De-Kassel.ogg"); in Germany, spelled **Cassel** until 1926[[3]](#cite_note-3)) is a city on the [Fulda River](/wiki/Fulda_River "Fulda River") in [northern](/wiki/North_Hesse "North Hesse") [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse"), in [central](/wiki/Central_Germany_(geography) "Central Germany (geography)") Germany. It is the administrative seat of the [Regierungsbezirk](/wiki/Regierungsbezirk "Regierungsbezirk") [Kassel](/wiki/Kassel_(region) "Kassel (region)") and the district [of the same name](/wiki/Kassel_(district) "Kassel (district)"), and had 201,048 inhabitants in December 2020. The former capital of the [state](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") of [Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hesse "Electorate of Hesse"), it has many palaces and parks, including the [Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe](/wiki/Bergpark_Wilhelmsh%C3%B6he "Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe"), which is a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). Kassel is also known for the *[documenta](/wiki/Documenta "Documenta")* [exhibitions](/wiki/Art_exhibition "Art exhibition") of [contemporary art](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art"). Kassel has a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [university](/wiki/University_of_Kassel "University of Kassel") with 25,000 students (2018) and a multicultural population (39% of the citizens in 2017 had a [migration background](/wiki/Migration_background "Migration background")).

**Kassel** (German pronunciation: [[ˈkasl̩]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Kassel.ogg "File:De-Kassel.ogg"); in Germany, spelled **Cassel** until 1926[[3]](#cite_note-3)) is a city on the [Fulda River](/wiki/Fulda_River "Fulda River") in [northern](/wiki/North_Hesse "North Hesse") [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse"), in [central](/wiki/Central_Germany_(geography) "Central Germany (geography)") Germany. It is the administrative seat of the [Regierungsbezirk](/wiki/Regierungsbezirk "Regierungsbezirk") [Kassel](/wiki/Kassel_(region) "Kassel (region)") and the district [of the same name](/wiki/Kassel_(district) "Kassel (district)"), and had 201,048 inhabitants in December 2020. The former capital of the [state](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") of [Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hesse "Electorate of Hesse"), it has many palaces and parks, including the [Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe](/wiki/Bergpark_Wilhelmsh%C3%B6he "Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe"), which is a [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). Kassel is also known for the *[documenta](/wiki/Documenta "Documenta")* [exhibitions](/wiki/Art_exhibition "Art exhibition") of [contemporary art](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art"). Kassel has a [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [university](/wiki/University_of_Kassel "University of Kassel") with 25,000 students (2018) and a multicultural population (39% of the citizens in 2017 had a [migration background](/wiki/Migration_background "Migration background")).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [Timeline of Kassel](/wiki/Timeline_of_Kassel "Timeline of Kassel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Ansicht_Kassel_%28Braun_Hogenberg%29_1572.jpg/250px-Ansicht_Kassel_%28Braun_Hogenberg%29_1572.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ansicht_Kassel_(Braun_Hogenberg)_1572.jpg)

Kassel, 16th century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Kassel-merian.jpg/250px-Kassel-merian.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kassel-merian.jpg)

A map of Kassel in 1648

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/KasselObereKoenigsstrasse2347.jpg/250px-KasselObereKoenigsstrasse2347.jpg)](/wiki/File:KasselObereKoenigsstrasse2347.jpg)

Königsstrasse, the main shopping street

Kassel was first mentioned in 913 AD, as the place where two [deeds](/wiki/Deed "Deed") were signed by King [Conrad I](/wiki/Conrad_of_Franconia "Conrad of Franconia"). The place was called *Chasella* or *Chassalla* and was a fortification at a bridge crossing the [Fulda river](/wiki/Fulda_(river) "Fulda (river)"). There are several yet unproven assumptions about the origin of the name. It could be derived from the ancient *Castellum Cattorum*, a castle of the Chatti, a German tribe that had lived in the area since Roman times. Another assumption is a portmanteau from Frankonian *cas*, meaning ‘valley’ or ‘recess’, and *sali* meaning ‘hall’ or ‘service building’, which can be interpreted as ‘(town) hall in a valley’.

A deed from 1189 certifies that Cassel had city rights, but the date when they were granted is not known.

The first castle in Kassel was constructed in 1277, later replaced by a Renaissance castle, the [Kassel City Palace](/wiki/Stadtschloss_Kassel "Stadtschloss Kassel"), which burned down in 1811.

In 1567 the [Landgraviate of Hesse](/wiki/Landgraviate_of_Hesse "Landgraviate of Hesse"), until then centered in [Marburg](/wiki/Marburg "Marburg"), was divided among four sons, with [Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Landgraviate_of_Hesse-Kassel "Landgraviate of Hesse-Kassel") (or Hesse-Cassel) becoming one of its successor states. Kassel was its capital and became a centre of [Calvinist](/wiki/Calvinist "Calvinist") Protestantism in Germany. Strong fortifications were built to protect the [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") stronghold against Catholic enemies. Secret societies, such as [Rosicrucianism](/wiki/Rosicrucianism "Rosicrucianism"), came to the rise, with Christian Rosenkreutz's work *[Fama Fraternitatis](/wiki/Fama_Fraternitatis "Fama Fraternitatis")* first published in 1617. In 1685 Kassel became a refuge for 1,700 [Huguenots](/wiki/Huguenot "Huguenot"), who found shelter in the newly established borough of Oberneustadt. [Landgrave Charles](/wiki/Charles_I,_Landgrave_of_Hesse-Kassel "Charles I, Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel"), who was responsible for this humanitarian act, also ordered the construction of the *Oktogon* ([Hercules monument](/wiki/Hercules_monument_(Kassel) "Hercules monument (Kassel)")) and of the *[Orangerie](/wiki/Orangerie_(Kassel) "Orangerie (Kassel)")*. In the late 18th century, Hesse-Kassel became infamous for selling mercenaries ([Hessians](/wiki/Hessian_(soldiers) "Hessian (soldiers)")) to the British crown to help suppress the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") and to finance the construction of palaces and the Landgrave's opulent lifestyle.

The [Brothers Grimm](/wiki/Brothers_Grimm "Brothers Grimm") lived in Kassel in the early 19th century, where they collected and wrote most of their [fairy tales](/wiki/Fairy_tales "Fairy tales"). At that time, around 1803, the Landgraviate was elevated to a Principality and its ruler to *[Prince-elector](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector")*. Shortly after, it was annexed by [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon_I_of_France "Napoleon I of France") and became the capital of the short-lived [Kingdom of Westphalia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Westphalia "Kingdom of Westphalia") under Napoleon's brother [Jérôme](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%B4me_Bonaparte "Jérôme Bonaparte") in 1807. The Electorate was restored in 1813.

Having sided with Austria in the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War") to gain supremacy in Germany, the principality was annexed by [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") in 1866. The Prussian administration united [Nassau](/wiki/Nassau_(duchy) "Nassau (duchy)"), Frankfurt and Hesse-Kassel into the new Prussian province of [Hesse-Nassau](/wiki/Hesse-Nassau "Hesse-Nassau"). Kassel ceased to be a princely residence but soon developed into a major industrial centre as well as a major railway junction. [Henschel & Son](/wiki/Henschel_%26_Son "Henschel & Son"), the largest railway [locomotive](/wiki/Locomotive "Locomotive") manufacturer in Germany at the end of the nineteenth century, was based in Kassel.

In 1870, after the [Battle of Sedan](/wiki/Battle_of_Sedan "Battle of Sedan"), [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") was sent as a prisoner to the [Wilhelmshöhe Palace](/wiki/Wilhelmsh%C3%B6he_Palace "Wilhelmshöhe Palace") above the city. During World War I, the German military headquarters were located in the Wilhelmshöhe Palace. In the late 1930s, Nazis destroyed [Heinrich Hübsch](/wiki/Heinrich_H%C3%BCbsch "Heinrich Hübsch")'s [Kassel Synagogue](/wiki/Kassel_Synagogue "Kassel Synagogue").

During World War II, Kassel was the headquarters for Germany's [Wehrkreis](/wiki/Wehrkreis "Wehrkreis") IX, and a local subcamp of [Dachau concentration camp](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp") provided [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour "Forced labour") for the [Henschel facilities](/wiki/Bombing_of_Kassel_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Kassel in World War II"), which included tank production plants.[[4]](#cite_note-4) There was also a camp for [Sinti](/wiki/Sinti "Sinti") and [Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") (see *[Romani Holocaust](/wiki/Romani_Holocaust "Romani Holocaust")*).[[5]](#cite_note-5) [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") prisoners of war from the [Stalag IX-A](/w/index.php?title=Stalag_IX-A&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stalag IX-A (page does not exist)") [POW camp](/wiki/German_prisoner-of-war_camps_in_World_War_II "German prisoner-of-war camps in World War II") were deployed to forced labour in the local arms industry in violation of the [Geneva Conventions](/wiki/Geneva_Conventions "Geneva Conventions").[[6]](#cite_note-6) The most severe [bombing of Kassel in World War II](/wiki/Bombing_of_Kassel_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Kassel in World War II") destroyed 90% of the downtown area, and some 10,000 people were killed and 150,000 were made homeless.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Most of the casualties were civilians or wounded soldiers recuperating in local hospitals, whereas [factories](/wiki/Bombing_of_Kassel_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Kassel in World War II") survived the attack generally undamaged.[[9]](#cite_note-9) [Karl Gerland](/wiki/Karl_Gerland "Karl Gerland") replaced the regional [Gauleiter](/wiki/Gauleiter "Gauleiter"), [Karl Weinrich](/wiki/Karl_Weinrich "Karl Weinrich"), soon after the raid.

The Allied ground advance into Germany reached Kassel at the beginning of April 1945. The US 80th Infantry Division [captured Kassel](/wiki/Battle_of_Kassel_(1945) "Battle of Kassel (1945)") in house-to-house fighting during 1–4 April 1945, which included numerous German panzer-grenadier counterattacks, and resulted in further damage to bombed and unbombed structures alike.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

[Post-war](/wiki/Post-war "Post-war"), most of the ancient buildings in the city centre were not restored, and large parts of the inner city area were completely rebuilt in the style of the 1950s. A few historic buildings, however, such as the [Museum Fridericianum](/wiki/Fridericianum "Fridericianum"), were restored.

In 1949, the interim parliament ("[Parlamentarischer Rat](/wiki/Parlamentarischer_Rat "Parlamentarischer Rat")") eliminated Kassel in the first round as a city to become the provisional capital of the [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany"), which the western city of [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn") won.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1964, the town hosted the fourth *[Hessentag](/wiki/Hessentag "Hessentag")* state festival.

In 1970, the Chancellor of West Germany [Willy Brandt](/wiki/Willy_Brandt "Willy Brandt") and the prime minister of the [German Democratic Republic](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") [Willy Stoph](/wiki/Willy_Stoph "Willy Stoph") met in Wilhelmshöhe Palace for negotiations between the two German states.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 1991, the central rail station moved from "Hauptbahnhof" (*main station*) to "Kassel-Wilhelmshöhe", the former now being used exclusively for regional trains. The city had a dynamic economic and social development in the recent years, reducing the unemployment rate by half and attracting new citizens.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Economy")]

Several international operating companies have factories or headquarters in the city (Volkswagen, Mercedes Benz, SMA, Wintershall, Krauss-Maffei Wegmann, Rheinmetall, Bombardier). The city is home to several hospitals; the public Klinikum Kassel is one of the largest hospitals in the federal state.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geography")]

Kassel is the largest city in the north of the [federated state](/wiki/Federated_state "Federated state") of Hesse in the south-western part of Germany, about 70 kilometers northwest of the geographic center of Germany.

It is located on both sides of the river [Fulda](/wiki/Fulda "Fulda"). Kassel's deepest point is in the north-eastern Fulda valley at 132.9 m above sea level.

The urban area of Kassel is divided into 23 local districts, each of which has a local council with a local mayor as chairman. The local councils are elected every five years by the population of the local districts. The local advisory board can be heard on all important issues affecting the local district. However, the final decision on a measure rests with the Kassel city council.

### Neighboring communities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Neighboring communities")]

Around Kassel is the [administrative district](/wiki/Districts_of_Germany "Districts of Germany") (*[Landkreis](/wiki/List_of_rural_districts_of_Germany "List of rural districts of Germany")*) of [Landkreis Kassel](/wiki/Landkreis_Kassel "Landkreis Kassel"). The following cities and municipalities border the city of Kassel (starting clockwise in the north): [Ahnatal](/wiki/Ahnatal "Ahnatal"), [Vellmar](/wiki/Vellmar "Vellmar"), [Fuldatal](/wiki/Fuldatal "Fuldatal"), [Staufenberg](/wiki/Staufenberg_(Reinhardswald) "Staufenberg (Reinhardswald)"), [Niestetal](/wiki/Niestetal "Niestetal"), [Kaufungen](/wiki/Kaufungen "Kaufungen"), [Lohfelden](/wiki/Lohfelden "Lohfelden"), [Fuldabrück](/wiki/Fuldabr%C3%BCck "Fuldabrück"), [Baunatal](/wiki/Baunatal "Baunatal"), [Schauenburg](/wiki/Schauenburg "Schauenburg"), [Habichtswald](/wiki/Habichtswald "Habichtswald"). Of these, Vellmar and Fuldatal in the north, Kaufungen in the east, Lohfelden in the southeast and Baunatal in the south are growing closer to the urban area.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Documenta_IX_Thomas_Sch%C3%BCtte_links.jpg/250px-Documenta_IX_Thomas_Sch%C3%BCtte_links.jpg)](/wiki/File:Documenta_IX_Thomas_Sch%C3%BCtte_links.jpg)

Installation by Thomas Schütte on the portico of the former [Residenzpalais](/wiki/Residenzpalais_(Kassel) "Residenzpalais (Kassel)") during Documenta IX, 1992

The first German [observatory](/wiki/Observatory "Observatory") was built in Kassel in 1558, and a later version from 1714 survives as the [Bellevue Palace](/wiki/Bellevue_Palace,_Kassel "Bellevue Palace, Kassel"). The *Ottoneum*, the first permanent German theatre building, was built in 1606.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The old building is today the Natural History Museum, and the now-called [Staatstheater Kassel](/wiki/Staatstheater_Kassel "Staatstheater Kassel") is located in a nearby building that was constructed in the 1950s.
Since 1927, Kassel has been home to [Bärenreiter](/wiki/B%C3%A4renreiter "Bärenreiter"), a music publishing house distributing compositions of several critically acclaimed classical musicians.

Since 1955 the *[documenta](/wiki/Documenta "Documenta")*, an international [exhibition](/wiki/Art_exhibition "Art exhibition") of [modern](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art") and [contemporary art](/wiki/Contemporary_art "Contemporary art"), has been held regularly in Kassel. The *documenta* now takes place every five years. As a result of the *documenta 6* in 1977, Kassel became the first city in the world to be illuminated by [laser](/wiki/Laser "Laser") beams at night (Laserscape, by artist Horst H. Baumann). This laser installation is nowadays still visible at weekends. Artworks from former editions of the *documenta* (mainly sculptures) can be found in multiple places in Kassel; among those are the "[7000 Oaks](/wiki/7000_Oaks "7000 Oaks")", a work of [land art](/wiki/Land_art "Land art") by the German artist [Joseph Beuys](/wiki/Joseph_Beuys "Joseph Beuys"). The latest/current edition of the *documenta*, known as "*documenta 15*", ran from 18 June until 25 September 2022.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Literature flourished in Kassel in the 19th century, when intellectuals gathered around the [Brothers Grimm](/wiki/Brothers_Grimm "Brothers Grimm"). Successful contemporary authors include Sebastian Mense ("Keilsberg")[[15]](#cite_note-15) and Maria Knissel ("Letzte Meile")[[16]](#cite_note-16).

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Climate")]

Kassel experiences an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfb*) close to marine climates, with a more notable [continental](/wiki/Continental_climate "Continental climate") influence than [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"). Using the 1961–1990 normal and 0 °C isotherm, the city already had a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (*Dfb*).[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-noaa-18)

Kassel has a population of about 200,000,[[21]](#cite_note-21) and is the 3rd largest city in [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse") state and the only large city in the North Hesse region.

Kassel is often called the city that located on the center of Germany due to its position.

Kassel first reached its first population peak of over 100,000 in 1899, and its second in 1943 with about 225,000. Kassel was destroyed during World War II and became an industrial city in 1950s. Today, Kassel is home to multiple companies and universities.

## Sights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Sights")]

The [bombing raids of 1943](/wiki/Bombing_of_Kassel_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Kassel in World War II") destroyed 90% of the city center. The city center was almost completely rebuilt during the 1950s and is a combination of renovated or reconstructed old buildings and architecture of the 1950s. Outside the city center, the suburbs are dominated by 19th-century architecture. Timber-framed old towns are situated in suburbs like Harleshausen and Bad Wilhelmshöhe. The oldest monument is the Druselturm; the Brüderkirche and the [Martinskirche](/wiki/St_Martin%27s_Church,_Kassel "St Martin's Church, Kassel") are also, in part, of medieval origin. The towers of the Martinskirche are from the 1950s.

### Churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Churches")]

#### St. Martin, Kassel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: St. Martin, Kassel")]

The main Protestant church of Kassel, it was begun in 1364 and finished in 1462. Severely damaged by British bombing in 1943, it was later reconstructed in a more modern style between 1954 and 1958.

#### St. Bonifatius, Kassel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: St. Bonifatius, Kassel")]

St. Bonifatius was designed and built in 1956 by [Josef Bieling](/wiki/Bieling_Architekten "Bieling Architekten").

### Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe")]

Main article: [Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe](/wiki/Bergpark_Wilhelmsh%C3%B6he "Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe")

The complex includes Wilhelmshöhe Palace (with the Antiquities Collection and Old Masters), the Hercules monument, and the Lions Castle.
[Wilhelmshöhe Palace](/wiki/Wilhelmsh%C3%B6he_Palace "Wilhelmshöhe Palace") above the city was built in 1786, by landgrave Wilhelm IX of Hesse-Kassel. The palace is now a museum and houses an important collection of Graeco-Roman antiques and a fine gallery of paintings comprising the second largest collection of [Rembrandts](/wiki/Rembrandt "Rembrandt") in Germany. It is surrounded by the beautiful [Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe](/wiki/Bergpark_Wilhelmsh%C3%B6he "Bergpark Wilhelmshöhe") with many appealing sights. The complex was named a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2013.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Wilhelmshoehe_-_Herkules_mit_Kaskaden.jpg/250px-Wilhelmshoehe_-_Herkules_mit_Kaskaden.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wilhelmshoehe_-_Herkules_mit_Kaskaden.jpg)

[Herkules Monument](/wiki/Hercules_monument_(Kassel) "Hercules monument (Kassel)") and water running down the cascades during the water features in the Bergpark of the [Wilhelmshöhe Palace](/wiki/Wilhelmsh%C3%B6he_Palace "Wilhelmshöhe Palace")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Kassel_asv2022-02_img07_Orangerie.jpg/250px-Kassel_asv2022-02_img07_Orangerie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kassel_asv2022-02_img07_Orangerie.jpg)

The [Orangerie](/wiki/Orangerie_(Kassel) "Orangerie (Kassel)") in the [Karlsaue](/wiki/Karlsaue "Karlsaue") park

The [Hercules monument](/wiki/Hercules_monument_(Kassel) "Hercules monument (Kassel)") is a huge octagonal stone structure carrying a giant replica of [Hercules "Farnese"](/wiki/Hercules "Hercules") (now at [Museo Archeologico Nazionale](/wiki/National_Archaeological_Museum,_Naples "National Archaeological Museum, Naples") in Naples, Italy). From its base down to Wilhelmshöhe Palace runs a long set of artificial cascades. Every Sunday and Wednesday afternoon at 14:30 (from May until October), the famous [water features](/wiki/Hercules_monument_(Kassel) "Hercules monument (Kassel)") take place, which start at the Oktagon, and during a one-hour walk through the park visitors can follow the water's way until they reach the lake of the Wilhelmshöhe Palace, where a fountain of about 50 metres (160 ft) marks the end of the features.

The *[Löwenburg](/w/index.php?title=L%C3%B6wenburg_(Kassel)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Löwenburg (Kassel) (page does not exist)")* ("Lions Castle") is a replica of a medieval castle, also built during the reign of Wilhelm IX. After the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") of 1870–71, [Napoléon III](/wiki/Napol%C3%A9on_III "Napoléon III") was imprisoned in Wilhelmshöhe. In 1918, Wilhelmshöhe became the seat of the German Army High Command (OHL); it was there that the military commanders [Hindenburg](/wiki/Paul_von_Hindenburg "Paul von Hindenburg") and [Ludendorff](/wiki/Erich_Ludendorff "Erich Ludendorff") prepared the German capitulation.

### Staatspark Karlsaue (Karlsaue Park)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Staatspark Karlsaue (Karlsaue Park)")]

The [Karlsaue](/wiki/Karlsaue "Karlsaue") is a large park along the Fulda River that is part of the [European Garden Heritage Network](/wiki/European_Garden_Heritage_Network "European Garden Heritage Network"). Established in the 16th century, it is known for the [Orangerie](/wiki/Orangerie_(Kassel) "Orangerie (Kassel)"), a palace built in 1710 as a summer residence for the landgraves. Today, the Orangerie contains the Museum of Astronomy and Technology, with a scale model of the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") spanning the entire park and beyond.
In addition, the Park Schönfeld contains a small, municipal [botanical garden](/wiki/Botanical_garden "Botanical garden"), the [Botanischer Garten Kassel](/wiki/Botanischer_Garten_Kassel "Botanischer Garten Kassel").

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Art museums")]

Europe's first public museum, the [Museum Fridericianum](/wiki/Fridericianum "Fridericianum"), was founded in 1779.[[23]](#cite_note-23) By the end of the 19th century, the museum held one of the largest collections of watches and clocks in the world.
Other art museums in Kassel include:

* [Hessisches Landesmuseum](/wiki/Hessian_State_Museum,_Kassel "Hessian State Museum, Kassel") (with a world-famous wallpaper collection).

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Sports")]

[Hessen Kassel](/wiki/KSV_Hessen_Kassel "KSV Hessen Kassel") is the [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club in the city, who plays in the [Hessenliga](/wiki/Hessenliga "Hessenliga") after being relegated from the [Regionalliga Südwest](/wiki/Regionalliga_S%C3%BCdwest "Regionalliga Südwest") in the 2017/2018 season. The city's own football stadium, the [Auestadion](/wiki/Auestadion "Auestadion") was built in 1953 and is able to hold 18,737 people. It is located in the south of Kassel at the quarter Südstadt, next to the Karlsaue.

Kassel has a long ice hockey tradition,[[28]](#cite_note-DW1-28) but it was not until 1977 that the Kassel ice rink (Eissporthalle) opened on a private initiative. The [Kassel Huskies](/wiki/Kassel_Huskies "Kassel Huskies") were founding members of the [DEL](/wiki/Deutsche_Eishockey_Liga "Deutsche Eishockey Liga") in 1994, belonging to the league from 1994 to 2006 and again from 2008 to 2010. In [1997](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_DEL_season "1996–97 DEL season"), they were runners-up in the championship playoffs, losing to [Adler Mannheim](/wiki/Adler_Mannheim "Adler Mannheim"), and reached the semi-finals on three more occasions. The Huskies ran into financial difficulties and dissolved in 2010.[[28]](#cite_note-DW1-28) The "Young Huskies", which is a junior and youth hockey club, decided to enter a men's team in the Hessenliga.[[28]](#cite_note-DW1-28) This is the fifth division and the lowest men's competition in the state of [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse").[[28]](#cite_note-DW1-28) The new club was expecting no more than 3,000 supporters for the first home game in the Hessenliga.[[28]](#cite_note-DW1-28) However, they had over 5,000 supporters come to watch.[[28]](#cite_note-DW1-28)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Kassel_asv2022-02_img46_Wilhelmsh%C3%B6he_tram_station.jpg/250px-Kassel_asv2022-02_img46_Wilhelmsh%C3%B6he_tram_station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kassel_asv2022-02_img46_Wilhelmsh%C3%B6he_tram_station.jpg)

A [tram in Kassel](/wiki/Trams_in_Kassel "Trams in Kassel")

Kassel has [seven tram lines](/wiki/Trams_in_Kassel "Trams in Kassel") (1, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8), with trams arriving usually every 15 minutes. The city also operates a light rail [Stadtbahn](/wiki/Stadtbahn "Stadtbahn") network called *[RegioTram](/wiki/Kassel_RegioTram "Kassel RegioTram")* using [Regio Citadis](/wiki/Regio_Citadis "Regio Citadis") low-floor trams which run on both tram and main line railway tracks with three lines (RT1, RT4, RT5). Moreover, a number of low-floor buses complete the Kassel public transport system. The introduction of low-floor buses led to the development of the [Kassel kerb](/wiki/Kassel_kerb "Kassel kerb") which improves the [accessibility](/wiki/Accessibility "Accessibility") at bus stops.

The city is connected to the [national rail network](/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn") at two stations, [Kassel Central](/wiki/Kassel_Hauptbahnhof "Kassel Hauptbahnhof"), and [Kassel-Wilhelmshöhe](/wiki/Kassel-Wilhelmsh%C3%B6he_railway_station "Kassel-Wilhelmshöhe railway station"). The traditional central station (Hauptbahnhof) has been reduced to the status of a regional station since the opening of the [Hanover-Würzburg high-speed rail line](/wiki/Hanover-W%C3%BCrzburg_high-speed_rail_line "Hanover-Würzburg high-speed rail line") in 1991 and its station (Kassel-Wilhelmshöhe) on the high-speed line at which the [InterCityExpress](/wiki/InterCityExpress "InterCityExpress") (ICE) and [InterCity](/wiki/InterCity "InterCity") services call as well as [Nightjet](/wiki/Nightjet "Nightjet") and [Flixtrain](/wiki/Flixtrain "Flixtrain").

Kassel is connected to the motorways [A 7](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_7 "Bundesautobahn 7"), [A 49](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_49 "Bundesautobahn 49") and [A 44](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_44 "Bundesautobahn 44").

The city is served by [Kassel Calden Airport](/wiki/Kassel_Calden_Airport "Kassel Calden Airport"). However, the airport only provides routes to limited destinations. The nearest airports to Kassel are [Paderborn Lippstadt Airport](/wiki/Paderborn_Lippstadt_Airport "Paderborn Lippstadt Airport"), [Hannover Airport](/wiki/Hannover_Airport "Hannover Airport"), [Dortmund Airport](/wiki/Dortmund_Airport "Dortmund Airport") and [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport").

### University of Kassel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: University of Kassel")]

The [University of Kassel](/wiki/University_of_Kassel "University of Kassel") is a public higher education institution and was founded in 1971 as a so-called reform university. It is the newest university in the state of [Hessen](/wiki/Hessen "Hessen") and has an urban inner-city campus between the city center and the Northern city district. There were around 25,000 students enrolled at the university in 2018, 3,359 of them non-Germans.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Two hundred and twenty-four students obtained their doctorate from the university in 2017.

The university offers a range of study programs, and several English master's programs as well as two short-term international programs, the [Summer University](/wiki/International_Summer_University_(ISU)_Kassel "International Summer University (ISU) Kassel") and the [Winter University](/wiki/International_Winter_University_(IWU)_Kassel "International Winter University (IWU) Kassel"). The [Kunsthochschule Kassel](/wiki/Kunsthochschule_Kassel "Kunsthochschule Kassel") (University of Fine Arts) is also part of the university with a satellite campus directly at the Karlsaue park in the Southern city district.

### Royalty and socialites

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kassel&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Royalty and socialites")]

* [Jérôme Bonaparte](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%B4me_Bonaparte "Jérôme Bonaparte") (1784–1860), Prince, brother of [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte"), lived in Kassel while he was king of [Westphalia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Westphalia "Kingdom of Westphalia")
* [Maria Amalia of Courland](/wiki/Maria_Amalia_of_Courland "Maria Amalia of Courland") (1653–1711), noblewoman, participated in creation of park at [Karlsaue](/wiki/Karlsaue "Karlsaue")
* [Prince Charles of Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Prince_Charles_of_Hesse-Kassel "Prince Charles of Hesse-Kassel") (1744–1836)
* [Prince Frederick of Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Prince_Frederick_of_Hesse-Kassel "Prince Frederick of Hesse-Kassel") (1747–1837)
* [Princess and Landgravine Augusta of Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Princess_Augusta_of_Hesse-Kassel "Princess Augusta of Hesse-Kassel") (1797–1889), consort to [Prince Adolphus, Duke of Cambridge](/wiki/Prince_Adolphus,_Duke_of_Cambridge "Prince Adolphus, Duke of Cambridge")
* [Landgravine Charlotte of Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Landgravine_Charlotte_of_Hesse-Kassel "Landgravine Charlotte of Hesse-Kassel") (1627–1686), noblewoman, member of the [House of Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/House_of_Hesse-Kassel "House of Hesse-Kassel")
* [Louise of Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Louise_of_Hesse-Kassel "Louise of Hesse-Kassel") (1817–1898), princess of Hesse-Kassel, later queen consort of [King Christian IX](/wiki/Christian_IX_of_Denmark "Christian IX of Denmark") of [Denmark](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Denmark "Kingdom of Denmark")
* [William IV, Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/William_IV,_Landgrave_of_Hesse-Kassel "William IV, Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel") (1532–1592), the first [Landgrave](/wiki/Landgrave "Landgrave") of the [Landgraviate of Hesse-Kassel](/wiki/Landgraviate_of_Hesse-Kassel "Landgraviate of Hesse-Kassel")
* [Frederick I of Sweden](/wiki/Frederick_I_of_Sweden "Frederick I of Sweden") (1676–1751), King of Sweden and also Landgrave of Hesse-Kassel
